content.text: Patch embeddings are the step that turns an image into a sequence of tokens so a Vision Transformer can process it like text. Each fixed-size image patch is flattened and projected into a learned vector, and positional information is added so the model knows where each patch came from in the image.
What patch embeddings do

A standard ViT does not look at an image as a 2D grid the way a CNN does. Instead, it splits the image into equal-sized patches, such as 16×16 pixels, and treats each patch as one token in a sequence.
Those patch tokens are then fed into the Transformer encoder, where self-attention can model relationships between distant regions of the image.
How the process works
The workflow is simple:
Split the image into non-overlapping patches.
Flatten each patch into a vector.
Apply a linear projection, or sometimes a small convolution, to map each patch into the model’s embedding dimension.
Add positional embeddings so the sequence preserves spatial order.
Feed the resulting token sequence into the Transformer encoder.
For a 224×224 image with 16×16 patches, you get 14×14=196 patch tokens.
This token count matters because self-attention cost grows quickly as the number of tokens increases.
Why it matters

Patch embeddings are the bridge between images and Transformers. Without them, the model would not have a token sequence to operate on, and it would lose the spatial structure needed for vision tasks.
They also control a major trade-off: smaller patches preserve more detail but increase compute, while larger patches are cheaper but may miss fine-grained information.
Mental model

A useful way to think about patch embeddings is as “word pieces for images.” Just as a language model reads a sentence as a sequence of word tokens, ViT reads an image as a sequence of patch tokens, each representing a local region of visual content.
Why positional embeddings are needed

If the model only saw a list of patch vectors, it would not know which patch was top-left, center, or bottom-right. Positional embeddings solve that by injecting location information into each patch token.
This is essential because images are spatial data, and location often changes meaning: an eye patch and a shoe patch are not interchangeable even if their raw pixel statistics look similar.
Design choices

The patch size is one of the most important ViT hyperparameters. - Smaller patches give finer spatial detail but more tokens and higher cost. - Larger patches reduce compute but can blur away small structures like text or tiny objects. - Variable-resolution methods and multi-scale patch embeddings try to improve flexibility when images do not fit one fixed size well.
In generative AI

In generative AI, patch embeddings are often the visual front end for multimodal systems. They convert images into a format that can be fused with language tokens or other modalities, enabling tasks like captioning, visual question answering, and image-conditioned generation.
That is why patch embedding is not just a preprocessing trick; it is a core architectural choice that shapes what the model can see, how much detail it retains, and how expensive inference becomes.
Common pitfalls
Using patches that are too large for the task, which can hide small details.
Forgetting positional embeddings, which removes spatial order.
Resizing every image blindly, which can hurt resolution-sensitive tasks.
Assuming patch embedding alone captures semantics; most meaning emerges later through Transformer layers.
Key takeaways
Patch embeddings convert an image into a sequence of learnable tokens.
d2l
They make ViT possible by adapting images to Transformer input format.
Patch size trades off detail versus compute.
Positional embeddings preserve spatial layout.
In generative AI, they are a foundational step for multimodal vision-language models.
